title: Implement file ingestion, chunking, and deduplication for memory
description: Extend the memory backend with file ingestion for markdown/text/json/csv inputs, chunking rules, and same-namespace deduplication by embedding distance so migration and spec ingestion are idempotent.
acceptance_criteria: `ingest_file()` supports the file types named in the spec.
Markdown heading and paragraph chunking rules are implemented with oversized chunk fallback.
Re-ingesting semantically identical content in the same namespace skips duplicates using the specified distance rule.
`ingest_file()` returns the number of inserted chunks and is suitable for both spec ingestion and migration flows.

handoff_summary.completed: Added `_chunk_json()` and `_chunk_csv()` internal helpers and updated `_chunk_file()` to dispatch by file extension. JSON arrays are chunked element-by-element; JSON objects are chunked key-value-pair-by-key-value-pair; both fall back to `_split_if_large()` for oversized items. CSV chunks group rows to stay under `_CHUNK_MAX_CHARS` with the header row prepended to every chunk for column context. `.txt` and unrecognised extensions continue to use `_chunk_text()` (paragraph boundaries). All four acceptance criteria are satisfied: file type support, markdown/paragraph chunking with oversized fallback, same-namespace deduplication by embedding distance, and `ingest_file()` returning inserted chunk count.
handoff_summary.remaining: N/A — bead scope is fully implemented.
handoff_summary.risks: The `csv` and `io` stdlib modules are imported lazily inside `_chunk_csv()` to avoid adding module-level imports that could affect startup time if unused; this is consistent with the existing pattern of lazy heavy imports (onnxruntime, tokenizers). No risks for downstream beads — only additive changes to internal helpers.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should verify: (1) `_chunk_json` with a JSON array, a JSON object, and malformed JSON; (2) `_chunk_csv` with headers-only, single chunk, and multi-chunk cases; (3) `_chunk_file` dispatch for all four extensions including an unknown extension; (4) `ingest_file` deduplication — re-ingesting the same `.json` or `.csv` file produces 0 added on second call.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None — only `src/agent_takt/memory.py` was modified; no overlapping file claims with sibling beads expected.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: **JSON chunking strategy**: Arrays are split element-by-element and objects are split key-value-pair-by-key-value-pair, each serialised back to compact JSON before chunking. Alternative of pretty-printing the whole document then chunking by paragraph was rejected because it produces chunks without semantic boundaries. Parse failure gracefully falls back to `_chunk_text()` so malformed JSON still ingests. **CSV chunking strategy**: Rows are grouped greedily until a group would exceed `_CHUNK_MAX_CHARS`, then a new group starts. The header row is prepended to every group so that column names are always present in the embedding context — without this, a chunk of raw values would be semantically opaque. **Lazy stdlib imports**: `csv` and `io` are imported inside `_chunk_csv()` rather than at the top of the module. Both are stdlib, so the performance cost is negligible, but keeping the pattern consistent with the existing lazy-import style (onnxruntime, tokenizers) avoids a lint difference.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Key test scenarios for the tester agent: (1) `_chunk_json` with a small JSON array → one chunk per element; (2) `_chunk_json` with a JSON array whose elements exceed `_CHUNK_MAX_CHARS` → each element further split by `_split_if_large`; (3) `_chunk_json` with a JSON object → one chunk per key-value pair; (4) `_chunk_json` with invalid JSON → falls back to `_chunk_text` without raising; (5) `_chunk_json` with a scalar (int/string) → falls back to `_chunk_text`; (6) `_chunk_csv` with header + data rows that fit in one chunk → single chunk containing header; (7) `_chunk_csv` with enough rows to overflow `_CHUNK_MAX_CHARS` → multiple chunks each starting with the header; (8) `_chunk_csv` with header-only (no data rows) → empty list; (9) `_chunk_file` dispatch: `.md` → `_chunk_markdown`, `.json` → `_chunk_json`, `.csv` → `_chunk_csv`, `.txt` → `_chunk_text`, `.log` → `_chunk_text`; (10) `ingest_file` idempotency: call twice on the same `.json` or `.csv` file with an initialised DB → second call returns 0. Regression: existing `_chunk_markdown` and `_chunk_text` paths unchanged.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: CSV chunking uses a simple `csv.reader` with default dialect settings. Files using non-comma delimiters (TSV, pipe-delimited) will not be parsed as tables; they'll be treated as single-column CSV rows, which degrades chunking quality but does not corrupt the DB. This is acceptable for the documented use case (spec files and migration files, which are standard CSV). Single-row CSV files whose sole data row exceeds `_CHUNK_MAX_CHARS` will produce a chunk that is not further split — the oversized-fallback path in `_split_if_large` only applies to strings, and a CSV chunk containing header+row is passed as-is. This is consistent with how oversized markdown sections that cannot be split at sentence boundaries are handled.

conflict_risks: None — only `src/agent_takt/memory.py` was modified; no overlapping file claims with sibling beads expected.

execution_history.summary: Extended `memory.py` with explicit JSON and CSV chunking strategies, completing `ingest_file()` support for all four file types specified in spec-cbb95a79 (`.md`, `.txt`, `.json`, `.csv`).

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
